email.subject: Daily Journey: Walker Miles on www.skechers.com

review.raw_markdown:
---

# Walker Miles — skechers.com Mobile Journey Review
**Date: March 28, 2026 | Yesterday's Score: 3/10**

---

## 1. Executive Summary

Another rough day on the Skechers site. The Men's navigation has some genuinely good bones — finding Hands Free Slip-ins was easier than it's been — but three critical failures drag this down hard: I couldn't log in (a popup hijacked the attempt), the cart page loaded completely blank, and search results led with Women's shoes. As a 62-year-old Skechers Plus member shopping on my phone, I never felt recognized or welcomed back. The BOGO deal was consistently visible, which is a real plus, but you can't complete a purchase if your cart won't load.

---

## 2. Business Impact Score: 4/10

**Marginal improvement from yesterday's 3/10.** The category navigation is cleaner and Slip-ins are easier to find. But the core transactional loop — log in, find product, add to cart, check out — still breaks. A loyal customer who can't see their cart isn't buying anything.

---

## 3. What's Working

**BOGO 50% OFF is plastered everywhere.** Every single screen had that blue banner across the top. Good. As a price-conscious shopper, I notice that. It made me feel like I was catching a real deal.

**Men's category navigation is solid.** The MEN menu was clean and readable — big text, generous tap targets, logical order. And "Hands Free Slip-ins" was right there as a quick link at the bottom of the menu, no digging required. That's exactly what I want to see.

**The Slip-ins category page delivered.** 258 results, clear heading, a nice description of the technology, and width options (Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit) front and center. Skechers knows their comfort-tech customers care about fit width. That felt right.

**Product page was clear and honest.** The Max Cushioning Arch Fit 2.0 at $135 — price was big and readable, the BOGO reminder was right there, the Hands Free Slip-ins badge was visible. No confusion about what I was looking at.

**ADD TO CART button is excellent.** Full-width, solid blue, big text. Easy to hit with a thumb. No complaints there.

---

## 4. What's Weak

**Login was completely blocked.** When I tried to sign in to my Skechers Plus account, an email capture popup — "Unlock 20% OFF" — appeared and ate my tap. The system timed out trying to get through to the login button. I'm already a member. Throwing a "sign up for discounts" modal in front of me is tone-deaf and technically broken. This is a returning loyal customer being blocked at the door.

**Zero personalization throughout.** Not one single screen acknowledged that I'm a Skechers Plus member. No "Welcome back, Walker." No member-exclusive deals called out. The homepage hero was a multigenerational family photo pushing a generic "Spring Shopping Guide." Where's the men's comfort content? Where are my Slip-ins front and center?

**Cart page was a blank white screen.** Completely empty. Nothing. This is a critical failure — if I'd made it through the size selection and tapped ADD TO CART, I would have landed on a white void and assumed the site was broken. That's a lost sale and probably a lost customer.

**Search showed Women's shoes first.** I searched "slip in" and 1,017 results came up — which sounds great — but the first two products I saw were both labeled "Women's." On a search with no gender filter applied, showing Women's items to a logged-out (or logged-in-but-unrecognized) male shopper is a missed opportunity. Men's results should surface first or at least equally.

**Login popup is hostile to existing members.** The 20% OFF email capture popup has a tiny X in the top right corner. Fine for dismissing, but it derailed the entire login attempt. There's no smart logic distinguishing "new visitor who might convert" from "existing member trying to access their account."

---

## 5. Recommendations

1. **Suppress the email capture popup for Skechers Plus members (or for anyone clicking the login button).** If someone is trying to log in, they're already in the funnel. Don't interrupt them with acquisition messaging.

2. **Fix the cart page — urgently.** A blank cart is a conversion killer. Whether it's a rendering bug, a JavaScript failure, or a session issue, this needs to be reproduced and resolved as a P1.

3. **Apply gender context to search results.** Use browse history, account data, or even just session signals to prioritize Men's results when a male member searches "slip in."

4. **Add a returning-member welcome moment.** Even a simple "Welcome back! Your Plus benefits are active" banner on the homepage login state would go a long way for loyalty.

5. **Feature Slip-ins on the homepage hero.** Not just in the sub-nav — put them in the main hero banner. This is the flagship product for my demographic. Own it.

---

## 6. Bottom Line

The category navigation is genuinely better than it was, and the BOGO deal visibility deserves credit. But I still can't log in cleanly, my cart was an empty white page, and the site doesn't know or care that I'm a loyal Plus member. A score of 4/10 — one point up from yesterday, mainly for the improved Men's nav — but this site is still failing its most valuable customers at the exact moments that matter most.

---

## 7. Evidence

**Step 1 — Homepage**
Large hero image of a family in nature, "SPRING SHOPPING GUIDE / SHOP NOW" button. Clean header with BOGO banner. Text was readable without zooming. However — nothing men's, nothing Slip-ins, nothing loyalty-focused. Felt like I landed on the wrong part of the store. No personalization whatsoever.

**Step 2 — Dismiss Popups**
Homepage reloaded cleanly. Banner updated to "Free Shipping with Skechers Plus — Join Now for free." Good that the free shipping perk is surfaced. But as an existing member, "Join Now" feels odd — I'm already in. Minor friction.

**Step 3 — Log In (FAILED)**
Instead of a login form, I got hit with a full-screen "Unlock 20% OFF" email capture modal. Big bold text, email field, CONTINUE button — fine design, wrong timing. The system couldn't reach the login button behind it and timed out. As a 62-year-old, I would have closed this popup manually, possibly lost my spot, and felt mildly annoyed. Login never happened.

**Step 4 — Men's Category**
This was the highlight of the journey. Clean mobile menu: MEN > Shoes, Shop by Activity, Comfort Technologies, Collections, etc. And right at the bottom of the list: "Hands Free Slip-ins" as a standalone quick link. That's exactly right. Large enough text, easy tap targets. A 62-year-old can navigate this without reading glasses.

**Step 5 — Men's Shoes / Hands Free Slip-ins**
258 results, clear page header, good technology description. Width filter buttons (Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit, Medium) are immediately accessible — that matters for comfort shoppers. Products starting to appear at the bottom of the viewport. Functional and well-organized.

**Step 6 — View Product**
Max Cushioning Arch Fit 2.0 at $135. Four stars, 8 reviews (light review count for a flagship style). Price was prominent. BOGO reminder in red text below the price — eye-catching. Hands Free Slip-ins badge visible on the product image. Left/right navigation arrows for photo gallery. All readable, no zooming required.

**Step 7 — Add to Cart**
Size grid showed all sizes from 7.0 to 14.0 — good coverage. Grid cells were a reasonable tap size. "Please select a size" warning appeared in red because no size was pre-selected. QTY dropdown showed 1. Full-width ADD TO CART button at the bottom — this is done right. Width selector was not visible in this view, which is a gap for comfort shoppers who need wide widths.

**Step 8 — View Cart**
Blank. Completely white screen. No items, no header content, no error message. This is the most critical failure in the entire journey. Whether caused by the size not being selected, a session issue from the failed login, or a technical bug — a blank cart page is unacceptable. A real shopper would assume the site broke.

**Step 9 — Search ("slip in")**
1,017 results returned — strong catalog depth. But the top two products displayed were both Women's: "On-The-GO Flex Radiant - Happy Day (Women's)" and "Contour Foam - Cozy Fit (Women's)." For a male shopper with no active gender filter, this is a miss. Filter and Sort controls were visible and accessible. The search *worked* technically, but the relevance ranking failed the user.

---

## Technical Audit

## Technical Summary

Site runs on Salesforce Commerce Cloud (Demandware). The journey surfaces three distinct technical failure classes: Kasada bot-protection misconfiguration causing 429s across the checkout path, a Stylitics CORS breakage on the product page, and 33 axe violations concentrated on the product detail and homepage. TTFB is generally acceptable (36–52ms) except the cart page (149ms). LCP and CLS are not captured, leaving Web Vitals incomplete.

---

## Accessibility

**Critical (3 violation types)**
- `aria-required-parent` (Homepage) — ARIA role elements missing required ancestor roles. Likely a carousel or listbox implementation with broken DOM nesting.
- `aria-required-children` (View Product) — Parent roles missing required child roles; widget structure is malformed.
- `aria-valid-attr-value` (View Product) — ARIA attributes with invalid values (e.g. `aria-labelledby` pointing to non-existent IDs).
- `button-name` (View Product) — Icon-only buttons with no accessible label. Likely size selector or image carousel controls.

**Serious (5 violation types)**
- `aria-hidden-focus` (Homepage) — Focusable elements inside `aria-hidden` containers; keyboard/screen reader users can tab into invisible content.
- `link-name` (Homepage) — Anchor elements with no text content or `aria-label` (likely image-only links).
- `aria-dialog-name` (View Product) — Modal/overlay dialogs missing accessible names; screen readers cannot identify dialog purpose.
- `aria-input-field-name` (View Product) — Custom ARIA input fields without labels.
- `link-in-text-block` (Men's Shoes) — In-text links distinguished only by color; fails for color-blind users and WCAG 1.4.1.

**Moderate**
- `landmark-main-is-top-level`, `landmark-no-duplicate-main`, `landmark-unique` (Homepage) — Multiple `<main>` landmarks and non-top-level landmark nesting. Suggests template injection or partial rendering appending duplicate page regions.
- `heading-order` (Homepage) — Non-sequential heading levels (e.g. `h1` → `h3`).
- `region` (Homepage, Men's Shoes) — Content outside any landmark region.

Total: 33 violations across 4 pages. Product detail page has the highest concentration of critical/serious issues.

---

## SEO

- `<html lang="en">` is set correctly on all pages.
- Heading order violations (`heading-order`) weaken document outline semantics used by crawlers for content hierarchy.
- Two Google Analytics 4 property IDs are loaded on every page: `G-XZTV9LQ9DQ` and `G-1234567890`. The latter is a placeholder/test ID pattern — if actively firing, it pollutes no-op GA4 property and indicates a misconfigured GTM tag that should be investigated.
- Yahoo `ytc.js` script is injected **twice** on Men's Category and other pages (two identical `vice-managed-src` tags). Duplicate pixel fires skew attribution data.
- Monetate loads via both `se.monetate.net/js/3/...` (versioned) and `se.monetate.net/js/2/...` (entry) — parallel load of two Monetate bundles on every page.

---

## Performance

| Page | TTFB | LCP | CLS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homepage | 36ms | — | — |
| Men's Category | 52ms | — | — |
| Men's Shoes | 41ms | — | — |
| View Product | 44ms | — | — |
| Add to Cart | 44ms | — | — |
| View Cart | **149ms** | — | — |
| Search | 40ms | — | — |

- TTFB is within acceptable range on all pages except **Cart (149ms)**, which is ~3.5× the site baseline. This is likely server-side session/cart state computation but warrants investigation.
- LCP and CLS were not captured. Without these, no Core Web Vitals assessment is possible — instrumentation gap needs fixing.
- Third-party script count per page exceeds 20 distinct origins (TikTok ×3, GTM, GA4 ×2, Pinterest, Reddit, Bing, Yahoo ×2, Snapchat, Facebook, Taboola, NextDoor, Quantum Metric, Bloomreach, Impact/TUNE, Monetate ×2, Stape, TrustArc, Stylitics, WebFontLoader). Even with `async`, parse/execute cost on mobile CPUs is significant and will degrade LCP and TBT.
- WebFontLoader pulls Mulish in 5 weights (n3–n7) — consider whether all five are used; each is an additional request.

---

## Mobile Optimization

- `--vh: 6.640000000000001px` CSS variable is set via JS to work around the iOS `100vh` browser chrome bug. The floating point precision (`.6640000000000001`) is a JS arithmetic artifact from `window.innerHeight / 100` — functionally harmless but suggests no rounding.
- `wf-mulish-*` classes on `<html>` are applied by WebFontLoader while fonts load, which is correct FOUT handling. No issue here.
- No `viewport` meta tag visible in the truncated HTML samples — cannot confirm `width=device-width, initial-scale=1` is set. This must be verified; its absence would break all mobile scaling.
- `--vh` being set at all confirms the site is doing client-side mobile viewport compensation, which implies layout is partially driven by JS rather than CSS alone — potential for layout instability on slow connections (contributing to CLS).

---

## Console & Network Errors

**429 Rate Limiting — Kasada bot protection (5 hits on `/fp?x-kpsdk-v=j-1.2.291`)**

The URL pattern `149e9513-01fa-4fb0-aad4-566afd725d1b/2d206a39-8ed7-437e-a3be-862e0f06eea3/fp` is a Kasada (KPSDK) fingerprinting endpoint proxied under the skechers.com domain. Receiving 429s on Kasada's own fingerprinting endpoint indicates the anti-bot layer is rate-limiting legitimate browser sessions during token acquisition. Downstream API calls that require a valid Kasada token will fail or be challenged until a token is established. This is a Kasada configuration issue — rate limits on the `/fp` endpoint need to be relaxed or the token refresh frequency tuned.

**429 on `/cart/` (1 hit)**

The cart page itself returned HTTP 429. This is a critical checkout path failure — a user loading their cart is getting blocked by rate limiting. Whether this is Kasada triggering on the cart endpoint or a separate rate limit rule, it will drop conversion. Needs immediate investigation.

**CORS failure — Stylitics `web-assets.stylitics.com` (2 errors)**

```
Access to CSS stylesheet at 'https://web-assets.stylitics.com/style.css?...'
from origin 'https://www.skechers.com' has been blocked by CORS policy:
No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present
```

Both a direct `<link>` stylesheet load and an XHR fallback attempt fail. Stylitics (outfit/shop-the-look widget) is completely non-functional on the product page as a result. The fix must come from Stylitics adding `https://www.skechers.com` to their CORS allowlist, or Skechers proxying the Stylitics assets through their own CDN.

---

## Recommendations

| Priority | Issue | Action |
|---|---|---|
| P0 | Cart page returning HTTP 429 | Audit rate-limiting rules on `/cart/` endpoint; exempt authenticated or cookied sessions |
| P0 | Kasada `/fp` endpoint returning 429 | Increase rate limit on the fingerprinting endpoint or tune token refresh interval in Kasada config |
| P1 | Stylitics CORS failure | Add `https://www.skechers.com` to Stylitics CORS origin policy or proxy assets |
| P1 | Critical ARIA violations (button-name, aria-required-children, aria-valid-attr-value) | Audit PDP component tree; add `aria-label` to all icon buttons; fix widget DOM structure |
| P1 | `aria-hidden-focus` on Homepage | Ensure elements inside `aria-hidden` containers are not in the tab order (`tabindex="-1"` or removed from DOM) |
| P2 | Duplicate Yahoo pixel script tag | Remove one of the two identical `ytc.js` injections in GTM or template |
| P2 | `G-1234567890` GA4 ID firing in production | Audit GTM container for test/placeholder tags that were never removed |
| P2 | LCP and CLS not captured | Add Web Vitals instrumentation (e.g. `web-vitals` JS library) to all pages |
| P2 | Cart TTFB 149ms vs ~40ms baseline | Profile server-side cart rendering; check for synchronous session/inventory lookups |
| P3 | `link-name` and `link-in-text-block` violations | Add `aria-label` to image-only anchor tags; add underline or non-color indicator to inline links |
| P3 | Duplicate `<main>` landmark and landmark nesting | Audit template composition — likely a partial being injected inside an existing landmark context |
